tens.desc: Core annotation usage and type specificity.
tens.label: Basics

rule.desc: Always use type hints for function parameters, return values, and class attributes.

tens.desc: Core annotation usage and type specificity.
tens.label: Basics

rule.desc: Use the most specific type possible. Prefer `Sequence[str]` over `list`, `Mapping[str, int]` over `dict`.

tens.desc: Core annotation usage and type specificity.
tens.label: Basics

rule.desc: Prefer Protocol over ABC for structural subtyping. Use ABC only when inheritance is semantically important.

tens.desc: Core annotation usage and type specificity.
tens.label: Basics

rule.desc: Use generic types for reusable components that work with multiple types. Prefer Python 3.12+ native syntax with automatic variance inference.

tens.desc: Core annotation usage and type specificity.
tens.label: Basics

rule.desc: Use type aliases for complex types. Use `TypeAlias` annotation for clarity.

tens.desc: Core annotation usage and type specificity.
tens.label: Basics

rule.desc: Use `NewType` to create distinct types from primitives when semantic distinction is important.

tens.desc: Core annotation usage and type specificity.
tens.label: Basics

rule.desc: Use appropriate collection types from `collections.abc`: Sequence, Mapping, Iterable, Iterator, Collection.

tens.desc: Core annotation usage and type specificity.
tens.label: Basics

rule.desc: Use `Literal` for values that must be specific constants.

tens.desc: Core annotation usage and type specificity.
tens.label: Basics

rule.desc: Use `Final` for constants that should never be reassigned.

tens.desc: Core annotation usage and type specificity.
tens.label: Basics

rule.desc: Avoid `Any` type unless absolutely necessary. Use generics, unions, or protocols instead.
Use modern union syntax `X | Y` (Python 3.10+) instead of `Union[X, Y]` or `Optional[X]`.
Use type narrowing with isinstance(), is None checks, and TypeGuard for complex narrowing.
Use dataclasses with full type hints for data containers.
Use Callable type hint for functions passed as parameters.
Use NamedTuple with type hints for simple immutable data structures.
Always specify return types, even for functions returning None.
Use ClassVar to annotate class variables (not instance variables).
Use `Self` type for methods returning the instance (method chaining, factory methods).
Enable mypy strict mode in your project configuration.
Avoid type comments. Use modern annotation syntax instead.
Use `if TYPE_CHECKING:` for import-only type hints to avoid circular imports.
Use forward references (string literals) for types not yet defined or circular dependencies.
Use `@overload` for functions with different signatures based on argument types.
Use ParamSpec to preserve function signatures in decorators.
Use Concatenate to add parameters to callables (useful for method decorators).
Enforce immutability through type hints (e.g., `Sequence`, `Mapping`, `AbstractSet`) rather than creating immutable copies of collections.
When using abstract base classes, use `metaclass=ABCMeta` with `@abstractmethod` decorator and ellipsis body.
Define contracts explicitly in code (class attributes with `__init__` methods, abstract properties) instead of documenting them in docstrings.
Never export TypeVar definitions in module __all__; TypeVars are implementation details, not public API.
Prefer public attributes for simple stored values over private attributes with `@property` decorators.
Use `__init__.py` to expose public API using `from ... import *`.
Define `__all__` in each module (not in `__init__.py`) to control the public API.
Be explicit when importing from other modules. Avoid `import *` in application code.
Use `_module.py` prefix for internal/private modules.
Constructor parameters define instance behavior and configuration; method parameters are call-specific data that varies per invocation.
Prefer absolute imports for public modules; use relative imports for private (underscore-prefixed) modules within packages.
Standard library, third-party, local. Alphabetical within groups.
Avoid circular dependencies. Use protocols or TYPE_CHECKING.
Import at module level by default. Use local imports only for optional dependencies or circular imports.
For untyped third-party libs, use stub files or `py.typed`.
Avoid namespace packages unless distributing plugins.
Prefer immutable data structures. Use `frozen=True` for dataclasses.
Use `frozen=True` for dataclasses that shouldn't change.
Functions without side effects, same input → same output.
Don't modify arguments or global state. Return new values.
Return new objects instead of modifying existing ones.
Use generators with `yield` for lazy evaluation and memory efficiency. Generators compute values on-demand rather than creating entire sequences in memory.

Prefer lazy evaluation over eager computation. Compute values only when actually needed. This reduces memory usage, enables working with infinite sequences, and can improve performance by avoiding unnecessary computation.
Use list/dict/set comprehensions for transformations.
Prefer comprehensions over `map()`/`filter()` for clarity.
Use functools for functional patterns: `reduce`, `partial`, `lru_cache`.
Use itertools for efficient iteration patterns.
Use `operator` module instead of lambdas for common operations.
Use `dataclasses.replace()` for immutable updates.
Never use mutable default arguments.
Use closures for encapsulation and factory functions.
Pass functions as arguments, return from functions.
Functions that take or return functions.
Use recursion for naturally recursive problems (trees, etc.).
Python doesn't optimize tail recursion. Use iteration instead.
Use `tuple`, `frozenset` for immutable collections.
Minimize global mutable state. Use parameters/returns.
Function calls can be replaced with their return values.
Chain transformations using function composition.
Consider Result/Option types for error handling (via libraries).
Use `functools.partial` to pre-fill function arguments.
Use reStructuredText format for docstrings.
Document all public functions, classes, and modules.
Every module has a docstring explaining its purpose.
First line of docstring is a brief summary, then a blank line, then details.
Type hints document expected types; don't repeat in docstrings.
Include usage examples in docstrings for complex functions.
Max 88 characters per line (Black standard).
Two blank lines between top-level definitions, one within classes.
4 spaces per indentation level.
Use underscore prefix only for internal implementation details. Attributes that represent observable state or configuration should be public with a read-only contract.
Avoid `__name__` (dunder) unless implementing protocols.
Consistent quote style. Double quotes `"` preferred.
Use f-strings for formatting, not `%` or `.format()`.
Use f-strings or `str.join()`, not `+` in loops.
Catch specific exceptions, not bare `except:`.
Create exception hierarchy for your domain.
Use `raise ... from ...` to preserve exception chain.
Use context managers (`with`) for resource management.
Use `contextlib.suppress()` for expected exceptions to ignore.
Use `finally` for cleanup that must happen regardless of exceptions.
Use `else` in try/except for code that runs only if no exception.
Define custom exceptions for your domain errors.
Pass useful information to exceptions.
Don't catch `BaseException` or `Exception` at top level unless re-raising.
Log exceptions with context before re-raising or handling.
Use assertions for impossible conditions (bugs), exceptions for expected errors.
Validate inputs and raise exceptions early.
Error messages should be clear, actionable, include context.
Use `ExitStack` for dynamic number of context managers.
Don't optimize until you measure. Clarity first.
Use profiler (cProfile, line_profiler) to find actual bottlenecks.

**Favor generators over lists by default.** Use generator expressions for large datasets to save memory and enable lazy evaluation. Only materialize to lists when you need random access, length, or multiple iterations.
Use dict lookups instead of long if/elif chains.
Cache expensive computations with `@lru_cache` or `@cache`.
Use `str.join()` for concatenating many strings.
Use `__slots__` for memory optimization in classes with many instances.
Local variables faster than global/attribute lookups.
Return early from functions to reduce nesting.
In hot loops, minimize attribute access.
Use built-in functions (C-optimized) when possible.
For numerical data, use numpy/pandas (vectorized operations).
Compile regexes used repeatedly.
Use sets for membership testing (`in`), not lists.
Import expensive modules only when needed.

tens.desc: Any overuse, missing hints, weak contracts.
tens.label: Missing or Weak Types

rule.desc: Using `Any` everywhere instead of proper types.
Functions without return type annotations.
Using `# type: ignore` comments instead of fixing root causes.
`cast(Type, value)` without runtime check.
`def func(x: int = None)` without `| None`.
`List[Union[int, str, dict]]` grab bag.
`Dict[str, Any]` for structured data.
Function parameters without type hints.
Returning different types without union.
`def func(items=[])` or `def func(config={})`.
Class claims to implement protocol but missing methods.
Covariant/contravariant used incorrectly.
`isinstance(x, List[int])` doesn't work.
Confusing sync/async in type hints.
`None | int` instead of `int | None`.
Using `@dataclass` decorator for classes with complex behavior, business logic, or methods beyond simple accessors.
Function modifies its arguments.
Functions read/write global variables.
Mutable class variable shared across instances.
Multiple references to same mutable object.
Modifying collection while iterating.
Property getter that modifies state or does I/O.
Returning mutable internal state.
Changing values marked as constants.
Singleton with mutable state.
Caching mutable objects.

tens.desc: In-place ops, loop mutation, frozen violations.
tens.label: Mutation Hazards

rule.desc: Modifying loop variable inside loop.
Mutating default dict values.
`list.sort()` instead of `sorted(list)`.
Working around frozen dataclass.
Dataclass without `frozen=True` when should be immutable.
Using file-level `# noqa` comments to suppress linter warnings across entire modules.
